bins.name: Define Jurisdiction Before Rulemaking
bins.desc: CEBA takes no position on whether FERC has authority over large load interconnections but insists the Commission must clearly define jurisdictional limits in any rulemaking. The filing warns that a 20 MW threshold would pull state-regulated facilities into federal oversight, and that behind-the-meter systems must be explicitly excluded.
quotes: if the Commission proceeds with a rulemaking in this docket, it will be critical for the Commission to explicitly seek comment on and clearly define the boundaries of its jurisdiction to ensure that any final rule rests on a solid legal foundation and is well-understood by industry stakeholders.

A 20 MW cutoff, for example, is too low: it would capture many mid-sized industrial and commercial facilities (factories, hospitals, campuses) traditionally under state jurisdiction, creating tension with state regulators and blurring wholesale vs. retail boundaries.

Pulling these systems into new transmission-level regulatory frameworks would create confusion, raise costs, and slow deployment—not only harming local reliability but also increasing systemwide energy costs.

bins.name: Raise Threshold to 75 MW
bins.desc: CEBA opposes the proposed 20 MW threshold as too low and recommends 75 MW instead. At 20 MW, the rule would capture mid-sized industrial and commercial facilities traditionally regulated by states, overwhelming FERC and creating wholesale-retail boundary disputes.
quotes: CEBA recommends substantially increasing the threshold to avoid overburdening the large load interconnection process. A new standard, such as a 75 MW threshold instead of 20 MW, would ensure that FERC is focusing its attention and resources on loads that have greater system impact.

A 20 MW cutoff, for example, is too low: it would capture many mid-sized industrial and commercial facilities (factories, hospitals, campuses) traditionally under state jurisdiction, creating tension with state regulators and blurring wholesale vs. retail boundaries.

bins.name: Support Joint Load-Generation Studies
bins.desc: CEBA supports jointly studying load and supply-side interconnection customers. Coordinated studies can identify more cost-effective transmission solutions and reduce the multiple-submission behavior that inflates queue volumes and delays both load and generator projects.
quotes: CEBA strongly supports the ANOPR proposal to jointly study supply-side and large-load interconnection customers where practicable.

bins.name: Voluntary Hybrid and Co-Location Rights
bins.desc: CEBA supports enabling hybrid and co-location configurations but insists they remain voluntary rather than mandated. Interconnection rules should let customers request only the injection or withdrawal rights they actually need, and study methodologies should reflect the reduced system impact of hybrid projects.
quotes: it is essential that interconnection design for large loads is structured to enable innovation and competition through voluntary pathways rather than mandates.
Hybrid projects can utilize generation and storage technologies that have a strong operational track record in terms of responding to balancing authority dispatch requirements.

The Commission should explicitly recognize that co-locating large loads with generation, whether existing or new, provides meaningful system benefits and should be treated as an authorized interconnection configuration and be studied in a way that reflects their reduced system impact.

bins.name: Standardize and Modernize Study Process
bins.desc: CEBA supports standardized interconnection study methodologies that model realistic large load operating behavior. Studies should incorporate advanced transmission technologies such as dynamic line ratings, follow Order 2023 precedent, and avoid queue-priority frameworks that undermine nondiscriminatory access.
quotes: CEBA strongly supports the ANOPR proposal to jointly study supply-side and large-load interconnection customers where practicable.

We recommend against introducing procedures which allow loads to 'skip ahead' either study or energization queues. 'Prioritization' frameworks introduce considerable room for fluctuating policy objectives to drive interconnection access and inevitably raise significant questions around non-discriminatory and non-preferential access.

CEBA recommends that the Commission require ATTs—including dynamic line ratings—to be evaluated alongside traditional upgrades for large load interconnections, consistent with Order No. 2023, given the significant benefits identified for consumers and interconnection customers.

FERC should also require transmission providers to study large-load interconnection requests based on realistic operating behavior, like the approach in Order 2023 for storage resources, to improve accuracy, avoid unnecessary upgrades, and reduce excessive costs.

bins.name: Cost Causation, Location-Specific Rates
bins.desc: CEBA supports a cost-causation framework where large loads pay for upgrades they trigger but share in broader benefits. Rates must reflect actual usage at specific locations, not broad averages, and studies should be based on realistic operating behavior to avoid unnecessary upgrade costs.
quotes: A cost-allocation framework grounded in cost causation is essential to preventing unjust and unreasonable outcomes. Large loads should pay for upgrades required for their interconnection, but should similarly receive the broader benefits that those upgrades may provide to other grid customers.

Any cost allocation rules that emerge from this proceeding should avoid setting load interconnection rates using overly generalized estimates or broad averages that bear little or no relationship to anticipated usage at specific locations on the transmission system.

FERC should also require transmission providers to study large-load interconnection requests based on realistic operating behavior, like the approach in Order 2023 for storage resources, to improve accuracy, avoid unnecessary upgrades, and reduce excessive costs.

bins.name: Recognize Co-Location System Benefits
bins.desc: CEBA urges FERC to formally recognize co-location of large loads with generation or storage as an authorized, high-value configuration. Co-location reduces congestion, line losses, and transmission upgrade costs. FERC should also complete its PJM co-location review and extend resulting tariff clarity to all markets.
quotes: Hybrid projects can utilize generation and storage technologies that have a strong operational track record in terms of responding to balancing authority dispatch requirements.

The Commission should explicitly recognize that co-locating large loads with generation, whether existing or new, provides meaningful system benefits and should be treated as an authorized interconnection configuration and be studied in a way that reflects their reduced system impact.
FERC should complete its review expeditiously and determine if PJM's tariff, as well as those of other electricity markets, need clearer rules to ensure grid reliability and fair allocation of costs.

bins.name: Voluntary Flexibility Pathways Only
bins.desc: CEBA supports interconnection designs that enable large load flexibility but opposes mandates. Most data center workloads are mission-critical and cannot be curtailed; any flexibility incentives must be structured as voluntary pathways to allow innovation without forcing operational risks on customers.
quotes: it is essential that interconnection design for large loads is structured to enable innovation and competition through voluntary pathways rather than mandates.

bins.name: Complete PJM Co-Location Review
bins.desc: CEBA calls on FERC to expeditiously finish its ongoing PJM co-location technical review and clarify tariff rules across all markets to ensure reliability and fair cost allocation.
quotes: FERC should complete its review expeditiously and determine if PJM's tariff, as well as those of other electricity markets, need clearer rules to ensure grid reliability and fair allocation of costs.

bins.name: Location-Specific Interconnection Rates
bins.desc: CEBA argues that load interconnection pricing should reflect usage and location to guide new load toward less-stressed grid points. Broad averages send no siting signal and can result in unjust cost allocations for both large load customers and existing ratepayers.
quotes: Any cost allocation rules that emerge from this proceeding should avoid setting load interconnection rates using overly generalized estimates or broad averages that bear little or no relationship to anticipated usage at specific locations on the transmission system.

bins.name: No Queue Prioritization Frameworks
bins.desc: CEBA opposes any mechanism that lets large loads skip study or energization queues, arguing such prioritization introduces policy-driven discrimination and violates open-access principles. Queue processes must stay grounded in technical and financial criteria.
quotes: We recommend against introducing procedures which allow loads to 'skip ahead' either study or energization queues. 'Prioritization' frameworks introduce considerable room for fluctuating policy objectives to drive interconnection access and inevitably raise significant questions around non-discriminatory and non-preferential access.

bins.name: Require ATT Evaluation in Studies
bins.desc: CEBA recommends that interconnection studies for large loads be required to evaluate advanced transmission technologies, including dynamic line ratings, consistent with Order 2023 requirements for generators. ATTs can increase capacity faster and at lower cost than traditional upgrades.
quotes: CEBA recommends that the Commission require ATTs—including dynamic line ratings—to be evaluated alongside traditional upgrades for large load interconnections, consistent with Order No. 2023, given the significant benefits identified for consumers and interconnection customers.

bins.name: Protect Behind-the-Meter Systems
bins.desc: CEBA urges FERC to explicitly exclude behind-the-meter and distribution-connected resources from any new large load interconnection framework. Pulling these systems into federal regulation would disrupt existing reliability benefits, raise costs, and slow beneficial project deployment.
quotes: Pulling these systems into new transmission-level regulatory frameworks would create confusion, raise costs, and slow deployment—not only harming local reliability but also increasing systemwide energy costs.
